text: The Youth Livelihood Programme (YLP) provides interest-free revolving funds to youth groups aged 18-30. Groups of 5-15 members can apply for UGX 5-25 million per project. The money is not a grant — your group must repay it so the next group can benefit. Apply through your Sub-County Community Development Officer (CDO). You need: National IDs for all members, group registration certificate, project proposal with budget, and a bank/SACCO account in the group name. Processing takes 1-3 months. The YLP has funded over 100,000 youth enterprises since its inception. Priority sectors include agriculture, agro-processing, trade, and services. Your group must show how the project will create jobs and generate income for all members.

Emyooga is the Presidential Initiative on Wealth and Job Creation. It provides UGX 30 million in seed capital to SACCOs (Savings and Credit Cooperative Organizations) formed around specific enterprises. There are 18 enterprise categories including: Bodaboda cyclists, salon operators, welders, tailors, produce dealers, restaurant owners, carpenters, market vendors, women entrepreneurs, youth leaders, and more. To benefit, you must form or join an Emyooga SACCO in your constituency with at least 30 members. The SACCO receives the seed capital and lends to individual members at low interest rates. Register with the Microfinance Support Centre (MSC). All members must complete financial literacy training before accessing funds.

The Uganda Women Entrepreneurship Programme (UWEP) provides interest-free revolving funds specifically for women's groups. Groups of 5-10 women aged 18+ can apply for UGX 5-25 million for projects in agriculture, trade, industry, or services. The programme targets women who are poor and unemployed or own micro-enterprises needing capital to grow. Apply through your Sub-County CDO — similar process to YLP. UWEP has particularly supported women in poultry farming, tailoring, salon businesses, piggery, crafts, and food vending. The programme includes a skills training component alongside the funding.

The Parish Development Model (PDM) allocates UGX 100 million per parish annually for enterprise development. As of April 2026, UGX 3.216 trillion has been disbursed nationally across 10,594 parishes. Eligible households in the subsistence economy form enterprise groups of at least 9 members and submit enterprise proposals to the Parish Development Committee. Before accessing funds, all beneficiaries must complete PDM financial literacy training (covering savings, record-keeping, and enterprise management). The money is a revolving fund — you repay so others can benefit. Priority is given to households in the lowest wealth categories. Parliament is reviewing equity concerns around distribution and repayment rates. The PDM aims to transform 39% of Ugandan households from subsistence to the money economy. Enterprise group structure is required — individuals cannot access PDM funds alone. Common enterprises funded: poultry, piggery, produce trading, tailoring, and food vending. Repayment rate remains a challenge, with some parishes reporting below 30% recovery. Ensure your group has a realistic business plan and repayment schedule.

Village Savings and Loan Associations (VSLAs) are the most accessible form of funding for youth entrepreneurs in Uganda. No registration required — just gather 15-25 trusted people from your community. Each member saves a fixed amount weekly (UGX 1,000-10,000). After 4-6 weeks, members can borrow from the pool at interest rates set by the group (typically 5-10% per month). At the end of a savings cycle (usually 1 year), the total pool plus interest is divided among members proportional to their savings. VSLAs build financial discipline, create emergency funds, and provide capital without bank requirements. Over 5 million Ugandans are in VSLAs. To start one: gather members, elect leadership (chairperson, secretary, treasurer, 2 key holders), write a simple constitution with rules, buy a lockbox with 3 keys (held by different people), and start saving.

The Youth Venture Capital Fund (YVCF) provides affordable loans to young entrepreneurs aged 18-35 through participating banks including Centenary Bank, Post Bank, Pride Microfinance, and FINCA Uganda. Individual loans range from UGX 100,000 to UGX 5 million, while group loans go up to UGX 25 million. Interest rate is 15% per annum — much lower than commercial rates of 25-30%. You need: a National ID, a business plan (HustleScale can help you create one), and some form of collateral or group guarantee. Apply directly at any participating bank branch. Processing takes 2-4 weeks. The YVCF is suitable for youth who already have a small business and need capital to grow, rather than those just starting from scratch.

BRAC Uganda provides microfinance loans specifically targeting women and youth in rural and peri-urban areas. Loan amounts range from UGX 100,000 to UGX 5,000,000, making them accessible for very small enterprises. BRAC uses a group lending model — you join or form a group of 15-30 members who meet weekly. Each member saves a small amount weekly (UGX 1,000-5,000) and after 4 weeks of consistent saving, you become eligible for a loan. Interest rate is approximately 25-30% per annum (declining balance), which is lower than most money lenders. Loan terms are 20-40 weeks with weekly repayments. No traditional collateral required — the group provides a social guarantee. To apply: visit your nearest BRAC branch office (branches in over 80 districts), join a group, complete the savings requirement, then submit a loan application with your business plan. BRAC also provides free business skills training, financial literacy, and health education to all borrowing members. Priority sectors: agriculture, food vending, tailoring, retail trade, salon services, and livestock. BRAC has served over 300,000 active borrowers in Uganda.

FINCA Uganda is a licensed Microfinance Deposit-taking Institution (MDI) regulated by Bank of Uganda, offering both individual and group micro-loans. Individual loans range from UGX 200,000 to UGX 10,000,000 for existing businesses with at least 6 months of operation. Group loans (Village Banking) are available for groups of 10-30 members with loans from UGX 200,000 to UGX 3,000,000 per member. Interest rates: 2.5-3.5% per month on declining balance. Loan terms: 4-12 months for micro-loans, up to 24 months for small business loans. Requirements: National ID, proof of business (trading licence or physical location), 2 guarantors for individual loans (or group guarantee for village banking), and a FINCA savings account with minimum balance. FINCA also offers: savings accounts (earn interest, no minimum balance), mobile banking via FINCA Mobile, and agricultural loans timed to planting/harvest cycles. To apply: visit any of FINCA's 30+ branches across Uganda or call their customer line. FINCA is a YVCF participating institution — youth aged 18-35 can access subsidised 15% interest loans through the Youth Venture Capital Fund channel at FINCA branches.

Innovation Village Kampala (located at Ntinda, Kampala) is Uganda's leading innovation hub offering startup grants, accelerator programs, and incubation support for tech-enabled and innovative youth enterprises. Programs include: 1) Startup Grants: Competitive grants of UGX 5-50 million for early-stage startups with scalable ideas. Applications open quarterly — follow @InnovationVillageKLA on social media for announcements. 2) Accelerator Programs: 3-6 month intensive programs providing mentorship, workspace, training, and seed funding (typically UGX 5-20 million). Recent programs have focused on agri-tech, fintech, health-tech, and climate solutions. 3) Incubation: Affordable co-working space starting at UGX 150,000/month with access to high-speed internet, meeting rooms, and networking events. 4) Partnerships: Innovation Village connects startups with partners like GIZ, UNCDF, Mastercard Foundation, and various embassies that run grant programs through the hub. Eligibility: Ugandan-founded startups, typically pre-revenue or early revenue stage, with an innovative product or service. Strong emphasis on technology-enabled solutions. To apply: visit innovationvillage.co.ug, attend their weekly open pitch nights (every Thursday 5-7 PM), or email applications during open calls. Innovation Village has supported over 200 startups, many of which have gone on to raise international funding.

The Mastercard Foundation Scholars Program supports young Africans with education and enterprise development through partnerships with Ugandan universities and organisations. In Uganda, the program operates through: 1) University Scholarships: Full scholarships covering tuition, accommodation, books, and living expenses at partner institutions including Makerere University and BRAC Uganda. Priority given to academically talented but economically disadvantaged youth, with emphasis on women and refugees. 2) Young Africa Works initiative: Partners with local organisations to provide skills training, mentorship, and enterprise support to help youth transition into dignified and fulfilling work. Programs target agriculture, digital economy, and financial services sectors. 3) Enterprise Development: Post-scholarship support includes business plan competitions, seed grants (UGX 2-10 million), mentorship networks, and access to the Mastercard Foundation alumni network across Africa. 4) Financial Inclusion: Partners with financial institutions to expand access to savings, credit, and insurance products for youth and smallholder farmers. Eligibility: Ugandan youth aged 18-35, with demonstrated academic ability and leadership potential. Applications open annually (typically January-March) through partner institutions. The program has supported over 50,000 scholars across Africa. To apply: check mastercardfdn.org for current partner organisations in Uganda, visit Makerere University's Mastercard Foundation page, or contact BRAC Uganda's education program. Applications require: academic transcripts, personal statement, evidence of financial need, community leadership examples, and two reference letters.

The UNCDF (United Nations Capital Development Fund) Agribusiness Grants programme aims to support up to 20,000 agribusiness micro and small enterprises (MSEs) in Uganda by November 2026. The programme focuses on women, youth, and refugees engaged in agriculture value chains — from farming to processing, storage, and marketing. Seed grants are available for qualifying agribusinesses, along with technical assistance in business planning, financial management, and market linkages. The UNCDF works through local implementing partners and financial institutions. Priority value chains include: coffee, dairy, oilseeds, grains, horticulture, and poultry. To access: contact UNCDF Uganda office in Kampala, apply through partner organisations (often advertised through district commercial offices and agricultural extension workers), or visit uncdf.org for open calls. The programme also supports digital financial inclusion — helping agribusinesses adopt mobile money payments, digital record-keeping, and e-commerce platforms. If your agribusiness involves processing or value-addition (e.g., milling maize, drying fruits, packaging honey), you have a stronger application. Include clear job creation projections and supply chain details.

The GROW Programme (Government of Uganda, accessible at grow.go.ug) is a government initiative specifically designed to support women-led enterprises across Uganda. The programme provides: 1) Business development services — training in financial literacy, enterprise management, marketing, and record-keeping tailored to women entrepreneurs. 2) Access to finance — facilitates linkages with financial institutions, government funds (PDM, UWEP, Emyooga), and grant opportunities for women's groups and individual women-owned businesses. 3) Market access — connects women entrepreneurs to buyers, helps with product quality standards, and supports participation in trade fairs. 4) Mentorship — pairs emerging women entrepreneurs with successful businesswomen for ongoing guidance. Priority sectors: agribusiness, textiles and garments, handicrafts, food processing, and services. Eligibility: Ugandan women aged 18+ who own or are starting a micro or small enterprise. Both individuals and women's groups can participate. To access: visit grow.go.ug, contact your district commercial officer, or visit any UWEP/PDM office for referral. The programme is active in all regions and works through existing government structures at the district and sub-county level.

The I&P Acceleration programme for Women Entrepreneurs in Africa (WE4A) supports women entrepreneurs in the green economy and climate-related sectors. The programme offers: 1) Acceleration — 6-12 month structured support including business model refinement, financial planning, governance improvement, and impact measurement. 2) Funding — access to investment-readiness grants and connections to impact investors, with typical ticket sizes of USD 5,000-50,000 (approximately UGX 18 million-185 million). 3) Capacity building — workshops on leadership, negotiation, fundraising, ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) standards, and scaling strategies. 4) Networking — connection to a pan-African network of women entrepreneurs, mentors, and investors. Focus sectors: renewable energy (solar, biogas, improved cookstoves), sustainable agriculture (organic farming, agroforestry, climate-smart techniques), waste management and recycling, water and sanitation, and eco-tourism. Eligibility: Women-led or women-owned enterprises in Uganda and East Africa with a business that addresses environmental challenges. Early-stage to growth-stage businesses. Must demonstrate both commercial viability and positive environmental/social impact. To apply: visit iandpacceleration.com or follow their social media channels for open application rounds (typically 1-2 cohorts per year). Applications require: business pitch deck, financial statements or projections, impact metrics, and founder profile. Strong applications show clear revenue model, addressable market size, and measurable environmental benefit.
